title: Multi-Region Active-Active Architecture on AWS Checklist
description: A build checklist for running active-active applications across AWS regions: traffic routing, multi-region data and conflict resolution, the application changes that make failover work, and the cost you sign up for.

items.title: Confirm you actually need active-active, not just multi-AZ
items.description: Multi-AZ already gives you 99.99% availability and survives a data center fire. Go active-active across regions only if you have a real reason: data residency law, an RTO under a minute for a full region outage, global users hurt by cross-ocean latency, or a contractual SLA. If none of those apply, you are paying roughly double to solve a problem you do not have.

codeBlocks.label: Decision triggers and a quick cross-region latency baseline
codeBlocks.code:
# Active-active is worth it only if at least one of these is true:
#   - regulatory data residency requirement
#   - RTO under ~60s for a full region outage
#   - global users hurt by cross-ocean latency
#   - contractual SLA you cannot afford to miss
# If none apply, stay multi-AZ and keep ~half your infra bill.

# Baseline the real round-trip between your candidate regions first:
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "us-east-1  total=%{time_total}s\n" https://dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "eu-west-1  total=%{time_total}s\n" https://dynamodb.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com

items.title: Pin down RTO, RPO, and your consistency model before writing code
items.description: Active-active forces a CAP choice. When the link between regions drops, you either keep serving and accept eventual consistency (AP) or block writes to stay consistent (CP). Most active-active designs pick AP because the whole point is to keep serving traffic. Agree the numbers and the model with the product team first, because they change every decision after this.

codeBlocks.label: region-targets.yaml agreed with product
codeBlocks.code:
# Agree these before any infrastructure exists.
availability:
  rto: "< 60s"          # time to recover after losing a full region
  rpo: "< 1s"           # acceptable data-loss window
  consistency: "AP"     # AP: stay available, eventual consistency
                        # CP: block writes during a partition
regions:
  active:
    - us-east-1
    - eu-west-1
write_strategy: "local write, async cross-region replication"

items.title: Make every write API idempotent
items.description: In active-active you will get retries, dual writes during replication lag, and clients that hit a different region after a failover. Without idempotency keys a payment gets processed twice and the customer calls support. If your write APIs do not accept a client-supplied key today, fix that before anything else.

codeBlocks.label: Idempotency key stored in a replicated table
codeBlocks.code:
# Every write API takes a client-supplied Idempotency-Key header.
# Store the key and its response in a replicated table until it is safe to expire.
def create_charge(request):
    key = request.headers["Idempotency-Key"]
    saved = idem_store.get(key)          # DynamoDB Global Table, replicated
    if saved:
        return saved                     # replay the stored response, do not re-charge

    result = process_charge(request.body)
    idem_store.put(key, result, ttl_seconds=86400)   # keep >= 24h across regions
    return result

items.title: Use globally unique IDs and kill auto-increment
items.description: Two regions accepting inserts will hand out the same SERIAL or AUTO_INCREMENT value to different rows, and replication merges collide on duplicate primary keys. Generate IDs in the app with UUIDv7 or ULID so they are unique without coordination and still sort by time, which keeps your indexes healthy.

codeBlocks.label: Drop SERIAL, use an app-generated sortable UUID
Generate time-ordered UUIDv7 in the app
codeBlocks.code:
from uuid_extensions import uuid7   # or a ULID library

new_id = str(uuid7())   # sortable + globally unique, no DB coordination needed
db.execute("INSERT INTO orders (id, region) VALUES (%s, %s)", (new_id, "us-east-1"))

items.title: Pick a data store that does multi-region writes
items.description: This is the hard part. DynamoDB Global Tables accept local writes in every region and replicate async in about a second, which is true active-active. Aurora Global Database replicates fast but only the primary region accepts writes, so it is active-passive for writes no matter what the marketing says. Choose based on whether you genuinely need multi-region writes.

codeBlocks.label: DynamoDB Global Table with a second active region
codeBlocks.code:
# DynamoDB Global Tables: every region accepts local writes, async replication ~1s.
aws dynamodb update-table \
  --table-name orders \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --replica-updates '[{"Create": {"RegionName": "eu-west-1"}}]'

# Aurora Global Database secondaries are READ-ONLY (active-passive for writes).
# Use it when you need a relational engine and can live with a single writer.

items.title: Decide conflict resolution per data type
items.description: Async replication defaults to last-writer-wins, and last-writer-wins silently throws away the losing update. Clocks drift, so the winner is whoever's NTP was faster, not whoever was right. Partition writes by key where you can so a record is only ever written in one region, use CRDTs for data that must merge, and keep an audit log so you can recover anything that gets dropped.

codeBlocks.label: Conflict strategy choices and an audit trail
codeBlocks.code:
# Async replication = last-writer-wins (LWW) by default. LWW drops the loser silently.
# Pick a strategy per data type:
#   1. Partition writes by key so a record is written from one region only (safest).
#   2. CRDTs for mergeable data (counters, sets, maps) - no lost updates.
#   3. LWW only where a lost update is genuinely acceptable.

# Keep an append-only stream so you can recover anything LWW discards:
aws dynamodbstreams describe-stream --stream-arn "$STREAM_ARN"

items.title: Route traffic with latency routing plus health checks
items.description: Route 53 latency-based routing sends each user to the lowest-latency healthy region, and the health check is what pulls a sick region out of rotation. Failover time is roughly check interval times failure threshold plus the DNS TTL, so keep the TTL short. If you need sub-30-second failover or non-HTTP traffic, put AWS Global Accelerator in front instead, since it fails over over the AWS backbone without waiting on DNS.

codeBlocks.label: Route 53 latency record tied to a health check

items.title: Build health endpoints that reflect real app health
items.description: A /healthz that returns 200 whenever the process is alive will happily keep traffic flowing to a region whose database is unreachable. The endpoint has to check the dependencies that matter and return non-200 when they are down, or Route 53 will never fail you out of a broken region.

codeBlocks.label: Health check that verifies dependencies

items.title: Make services stateless and externalize session state
items.description: After a failover, or just normal latency routing, a user's next request can land in a different region. Anything kept in process memory is gone. Use stateless signed tokens where you can, and a replicated store for carts and longer sessions so state survives a region loss.

codeBlocks.label: Replicated session table for stateful sessions
codeBlocks.code:
# The next request may hit a different region. Do not keep session state on the box.
# Option A: stateless signed JWT, validated in every region (simplest).
# Option B: replicated session store for carts / long sessions:
aws dynamodb create-table \
  --table-name sessions \
  --attribute-definitions AttributeName=sid,AttributeType=S \
  --key-schema AttributeName=sid,KeyType=HASH \
  --billing-mode PAY_PER_REQUEST \
  --region us-east-1
# then add an eu-west-1 replica the same way as the orders table

items.title: Handle data residency by routing to a home region
items.description: Some data legally cannot leave a region under GDPR, India DPDP, or China PIPL, which means replicating it active-active is illegal. Pin each user to a home region at signup, write and read their regulated data only there, and replicate globally only what is safe to copy. Route by the user's home-region claim at the edge, not purely by latency.

codeBlocks.label: CloudFront Function routing by home region

items.title: Replicate object storage across regions
items.description: Your app and database are multi-region, but uploads and assets in a single S3 bucket are still a single point of failure. S3 Multi-Region Access Points give you one global endpoint that routes to the nearest bucket and fails over fast. If you only need a backup copy, plain Cross-Region Replication is cheaper.

codeBlocks.label: S3 Multi-Region Access Point across two buckets

items.title: Deploy identical stacks to every region from one IaC module
items.description: Drift between regions is the most common reason a failover fails: the standby is missing a security group, an env var, or the latest app version. Define the stack once and apply it to every region from the same module so the regions cannot diverge by hand.

codeBlocks.label: Same module applied per region via provider aliases

items.title: Set up cross-region observability and replication-lag alarms
items.description: During an incident you need to see every region side by side and know which one is sick, so tag every metric and trace with its region. Just as important, alarm on replication lag. If lag spikes, your RPO is silently blown and a failover right then would lose data.

codeBlocks.label: Tag telemetry by region and alarm on replication lag
codeBlocks.code:
# Tag every metric/trace with its region so you can tell regions apart:
export OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES="service.name=api,cloud.region=us-east-1"

# Alarm on DynamoDB cross-region replication lag (RPO guard):
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name dynamodb-replication-lag-eu \
  --namespace AWS/DynamoDB \
  --metric-name ReplicationLatency \
  --dimensions Name=TableName,Value=orders Name=ReceivingRegion,Value=eu-west-1 \
  --statistic Average --period 60 --threshold 3000 \
  --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold --evaluation-periods 3

items.title: Automate failover and test it with regular game days
items.description: If you never test failover, the first real one is the test, and it will surprise you. Run a game day at least quarterly: drain a region on purpose, watch traffic move, and confirm the data layer keeps up. Test the failback path too, because coming home is where teams usually get burned.

codeBlocks.label: Force a region out and inject a real fault
codeBlocks.code:
# Pull a region out of rotation and watch Route 53 shift traffic:
aws route53 update-health-check --health-check-id "$HC_ID" --disabled

# Or inject a real network fault with AWS Fault Injection Simulator:
aws fis start-experiment --experiment-template-id "$FIS_TEMPLATE_ID"

# Always test the FAILBACK path too, not just the failover.

items.title: Watch the cost, especially inter-region data transfer
items.description: Active-active runs about 2.2x single-region once you count it honestly. The line people forget is inter-region data transfer at roughly $0.02 per GB plus replicated write capacity charged in every region. Put a budget alarm on it and watch the data-transfer line specifically so the bill does not surprise you.

codeBlocks.label: Budget guardrail for the multi-region account
codeBlocks.code:
# Multi-region is ~2.2x single-region. Inter-region transfer (~$0.02/GB) and
# replicated write capacity are the costs people forget. Put a guardrail on it.
aws budgets create-budget \
  --account-id "$ACCOUNT_ID" \
  --budget '{
    "BudgetName": "multi-region-monthly",
    "BudgetLimit": {"Amount": "8000", "Unit": "USD"},
    "TimeUnit": "MONTHLY",
    "BudgetType": "COST"
  }'
# Then watch the DataTransfer-Regional-Bytes line in Cost Explorer specifically.
